speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number four. An interview with Regina Mauskopf. You were describing the shower that you took when you first arrived in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, after that we went into those-- I mean, not to the barracks, as they say, but to those-- they had [INAUDIBLE] too, kind of, like on top. But just, like, one. In the bottom, everybody left their [INAUDIBLE]. It was just this. We were 16 of us in a place like this, like a square from-- a square, like through here. From the wall to here in a square. I don't know how many yards that would be.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's a very small place.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were 16 of us, and we just would sit there. And we were terribly thirsty. So they went and they gave us-- it was almost like just from hay-- a tea. The hay was swimming around, kind of. You could see there, kind of, that it wasn't-- not really a tea. I don't know. And it was bitter. Like, the bitter stink like whatever, but we were drinking it. And it was a drink-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were drinking it-- and it was one bowl, you know, like when you make some drink. I don't know. You know, round bowl. And that we-- all the 16 had one bowl, and everybody took a sip. And there was a girl, the poor thing, she had a cold, and it was draining from the nose, but that's what it was. And it came at night. There was no place we had to-- to-- to sleep. And out here, I had that 14-year-old. You know, she was skinny, like, like, like nothing. You know, because it was already so many weeks not to eat properly.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went-- as I say, my cousin, maybe sometimes I would not have been able, so she could get a lot of credit for helping me out. So we went, and we did like this. She stood there, and I stood here, and we spread our feet like this, and we let her sleep. See?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were standing, not sitting. Stand up, and that's how she took a nap. And then the other one, I don't remember if she wanted or not, but her I see there, laying between us. That's how she was sleeping, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was your niece, Leah?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my-- That was daughter, the little one.

speaker: interviewer
text: The little one.

speaker: interviewee
text: The 14-year-old. Yeah. And then-- I don't know. Then it started at, you know, like we had to go. We had to wait there, and there is a certain amount of girls, five or ten, because otherwise with the [INAUDIBLE] the Jewish girls were taking care on that inside to go. And then they start warning us, we should not drink the water there. And we're going to go because we're going to get-- I don't know, dysentery-- you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: Diarrhea?

speaker: interviewee
text: Diarrhea. We're going to get. We should not drink the water. So between that tea and then that there's no drinking water, and then the next day, we did got that started getting that soup. But we had been there three days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, if the girls were nice, and they would stay, you know, we were taking turns. Things. I don't remember-- I think she did one night from those three, four nights, my cousin. There were the shoes underneath. She just climbed down. It's like a bunk bed, to climb down with that little leather by the side. And she went down there. It was not allowed, probably. They would have seen her-- well, they would not have, you know, it's-- you know, it's the-- the-- the-- the law they had there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's how it was. There was about-- as I say, we were just there. [INAUDIBLE]. And then again, even for a-- like, for a shower. The fourth day. And as I say, we got civil clothes. Just kind of-- Civilian clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Civilian clothes, yes. Skirt, something with a blouse, and something just like that. And we were staying there, outside, waiting for the train out from the place there. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: All 16 of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. No. Well, I-- there are a lot of them. I don't know who went or who didn't went. I-- we were just looking for the four of us, and there was the fifth one, that she-- she passed away in Bergen-Belsen. Her name was Schindler, too, but she was Jewish. And so we were staying there, and there wasn't a-- the kitchen was not far away. Kind of was like, like almost at the street. You know, we were eating, there was the train, kind of thing. And then we heard kind of somebody start hollering Hungarian.

speaker: interviewee
text: Who is there from Polena? And that is where my brother was living. And right away, I said, I had a brother. And so-and-so, his name. Oh, she-- you know what that was? That was Barry's sister-in-law. Your husband's sister-in-law?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- yeah, my husband's sister-in-law. She was working in the kitchen. She started bringing buckets of food-- not just for us, whoever wanted there in that group. You know, kind of a-- till the train came.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had a good meal there. Well, anyway, and then when we went to Plaszow. And in the train, and as I say again, it was-- that-- you know, that was always the bucket in-- in the corner, in the train. And again, it was a cattle car?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Plaszow was in Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Krakow, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember how long that took?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not long. I don't remember. As I said, we were waiting for the train. It was early in the morning, because it was so cold, you know, kind of misty. And it-- no, it didn't take long, maybe half a day. Because sometimes the trains didn't go kind of like, you know, they were pushing back and forth the-- the-- the cars.

speaker: interviewer
text: By that time, did you know where your parents were?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No idea?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no idea. That's what we heard in Auschwitz. They said whoever comes out of the thing. Yes, well, what is it? What is it? They [INAUDIBLE] themselves. They made kind of like a laugh. You know, they were not laughing for-- for thing but, you know, that bitter laugh. The girls which knew already, there, you know, they were there already way before. And they were kind of the [INAUDIBLE], as they called them. They were in-- in the things. That's what it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you never saw your parents and the rest of the family since that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: No more. No more.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you arrived at Plaszow, you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you-- when you arrived at the train station, how did you get to the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not far away, somehow. I really don't know. As I said, I have a little [INAUDIBLE]. It seemed to me that when we left then, it was not far away. But I don't remember how we-- we came in. If-- if-- how far from the train it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's what we came in, there. But through the gate we came to up there where there was like a [INAUDIBLE] the zahlappell, because that was the-- the-- that's what they told us, that this was a Jewish cemetery.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we noticed afterwards that the walkway up to our barracks was kind of like a little sidewalk made, and it was from the-- from the headstones, with the rabbis and [INAUDIBLE] so-and-so and so-and-so. You know. So we used to sometimes, if we had time, we walked slowly, and we would just kind of start to read the names.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had to walk on those headstone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish headstones?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. Yeah, we walked on them. It was like a-- made like a sidewalk out of it. That's what it was. So but we were staying there on a zahlappell for quite a while because there was a big officer, a German officer in the middle with [INAUDIBLE]. And the other ones were running around, and they're, you know, because they were-- you know, like the put-- we had to put us there because it was not a real big camp. And-- and as we were Helmans, all, the four of us, and I was-- we-- we did always like this. She was first, my cousin--

speaker: interviewer
text: Her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dora.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dora.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and-- and the girls we let in the middle. And I was in the back, or sometimes I was in the front, and she was-- we were always shielding them. But they were-- we were going to see what's going to happen to us or what's going to happen behind us. And-- and when he called the names, because he had on the-- the things. He had our names on-- on a-- on a sheet.

speaker: interviewer
text: On a list. On a list

speaker: interviewee
text: On a list. I don't know from where or how it came. I don't know. It's still probably from home, somehow, the connection. I don't know. And as he-- he called a Helman, one. And she went already up on the other side-- we were already assigned to the barracks-- to wait. And then the two nieces. And in between them, a soldier came over and said something to him to the ear, you know. And then the soldier left, and as he looked in the thing, and he saw Helman, so he thought he is finished with the Helmans. And I'm standing there, and he doesn't call my name anymore. He calls out another initial. Everybody has left, went away, and I'm left there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he asked me, you know, what I'm waiting for. I said, they didn't call my name. They called my nieces. And then he looked in, and that's how I-- my nieces went to the other barrack there, all of them, with my cousin. And I was assigned already.

speaker: interviewee
text: They put me in right there to the block, to the 15. So I was separated from them. I thought, God knows, you know, how it's going to be there. Now, you know, we are not together.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went over to my-- that was like the next thing, not right away, because I had to see first what was going on there. And then they were kind of like uphill, just kind of like far away, like, maybe like a half a yard, I mean, like, you know. Up the yard, just kind of up the hill.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and then there was another woman, which somehow they got changed. The girl wound up with me, and the mother was in the fifth barrack, and she was in this one. And there she is crying after her mother, and here I'm crying the other way. So I went over to-- to the [GERMAN]. She was-- she was from [GERMAN] already, and she was nice. And-- and I told her the story, but I didn't tell her about the girl. I was talking for myself, not about them. And she said, OK, find out if somebody wants to change with you, then she'll come here, and you'll go there, and that's all. So-- but you have to go and talk to other [GERMAN] from the-- from block 5.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to her. She was not as nice as this one. And she said, OK. Talk over, again with Mrs. Finkelstein, and-- and we'll do it someday. And well, it started to be kind of like we started to work together. Well, not work. It was raining that day.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was just for-- for-- they just put us out like-- like we should be outside. We were digging, kind of, you know, where you put in-- in the yard. When you peel off the grass. I don't know how you call it. You know. Well, anyway, it was kind of lumps of that. So we took from one place and put it another, and from there to there, just for the sake of working, being outside.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we thought, we work together anyway, and we could see each other kind of after that. And we did not go to work at that time [INAUDIBLE] but after the zahlappell, we could sneak to each other in the barracks. And so we thought, well, all right. And I went a few times to the [GERMAN] and then we let it go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And well, I was assigned to work, and they were assigned to a different place to work. I was working in a-- in a quarry there. Out-- it was out of the gate. And-- and they were working-- I don't remember where they were working. And the poor thing-- that-- for-- for [INAUDIBLE] they're always-- oh, she lost that piece of bread that she got. She was so kind of helpless always.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is she?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dora, my niece--

speaker: interviewer
text: Dora.

speaker: interviewee
text: The 14-year-old. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this is physical work at the quarry for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work, exactly?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were those big pitchforks. But they were this wide, and the stones were kind of that size wide. And there were trucks. So with those pitchforks, we picked up those stones, and we were loading on the trucks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what it was used for?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know what it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many hours a day did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't know. I really don't know. We had lunch hour, which we didn't have nothing to eat anyway there on the lunch hour, but the Germans, they were sitting down. Then when [INAUDIBLE] Wehrmacht took us over from the-- from the gate to work there. And they were nice. They were eating their lunch and sometimes their half a sandwich. They kind of give one day for one, one day for another, and left a little bit in the thermos cup. A little bit of coffee, what they had. Whatever they had.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were not as kind of with the beating. Kind of-- we were kind of things. And well, I-- I did a little bit of business there with bread from the-- well, the peasants, they knew already from probably before. You know? But for me, told one, a woman, she was from Krakow, there, in the thing. And she asked me once where I'm working. And I said that I'm working out of the gate. She said, gee, if you work out of the gate, you could sneak in stuff, kind of. Like sometimes they get green onions, something like that, or bread. I said, well, I haven't got any money. I said, what kind of money?

speaker: interviewee
text: She said, this [INAUDIBLE]. I said, I haven't got any. She said to me, you know what, I'll lend you-- I don't remember if it's 10 or 20-- I'll lend you some money, and you buy whatever you could buy, and I'll pay for it, whatever the price that on the black-- that's black market inside. Whatever it is. And then you will have enough saved up, then you pay me back what I lent you. What I lent-- you know. So I-- I carried that in my-- I had-- that's another story too, from those shoes where I had. I had my brother's combat boots, which I thank for my father. And that's what he said when I was-- wanted to put on something, shoes like this. And they took us away from home, and she said, you're not looking now for fanciness. Then he came in, and he was looking over what I'm putting on and the shoes. So you know, that Friday, when they were taking us away. And he said, you just take-- and they were that-- the brother from Prague. And he didn't have the real big-- well, I put on two, three pairs of socks in it. And they were brand new, that thing. And I put on-- had the things, and I put-- toward the things, I had a little tiny scissors slipped in, that kind of like a nail, a manicure thing, and a needle with a piece of thread in it, and that was always in that lining in the combat boot. So I went and I found out there in the quarry from other ones where to put that money, you know, how to do it. And there, where the latrine was, there was a stone and kind of was kind of like a little cave underneath. You leave the money there, and then when you go later to the latrine, whatever you find there, that's yours. So there was always-- I was buying more kind of easy. You know, like an Italian bread, something like that. And that was hard to take in too. We had to make underneath the skirt kind of two loops tied to the-- to the belt and then switch in, kind of, that bread in those loops. And that's how I made a little bit of business. So we were not really already that hungry, because if you have a piece of bread, and then you could buy-- I bought once a-- a head of cabbage. Something green to eat. Well, that head-- head of cabbage did me so good. Because after that-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I mean, we stopped already going there. I worked already in a barrack which we were sorting. The girls were working that time with me to already [INAUDIBLE]. My nieces and my cousin. It was the military clothes which came torn or bloody from the front. We were selecting what could be repaired and what could be thrown out.

speaker: interviewee
text: So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when we were going-- it was-- that time it was [INAUDIBLE], and actually, we were fasting. And we came home from the zahlappell, and something was trouble there. Their counting was not kind of done the right, proper way. So we were staying very long. It started to get dark, and we were still in the zahlappell. And I have that cabbage with me. So I-- I don't remember if the girls wanted it or didn't want it. Everybody was kind of, you know, after a fasting, you know, fasting. You fasted of all the time, but. So I was kind of nibbling on that cabbage, and I got an upset stomach. I got sick. So but I went to work the next day and the next day, and then I was complaining for the [GERMAN] that somehow I don't feel good, if it's possible, I should stay, because I was not allowed to stay in the-- in the barracks. Just those stayed there which came from that [GERMAN], it called, from the hospital. So they stayed a few days till they were assigned somewhere else to go. And then-- so she said, if you want to go to [GERMAN], I'll give you a slip, and there and there it is, and I have it there on that piece of paper. So I went there, and we were kind of so established in the camp, so we were not be afraid that we were going to be, like, separated, because we could see each other. So I went there, and they-- every afternoon, after they came home from work, they came there by the fence. I was laying by the window because I was asking, could I have my sisters. I always said that they are my sisters. I never said that they are, you know, nieces or things. Just we are sisters. So I had a window there that-- to lay there. I got white bread, that was the diet, and some kind of white-- like navy beans, something like that for the food, not more-- more than the outside, but kind of better a little bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't do nothing. I don't remember if I got some aspirin or whatever. I don't remember what they did. What they did there in Krakow, they were taking blood from us in the barracks a few times, I don't know for what. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- and as I was there, and then when they start, the one day, the girls came to the window there and say, Regina, try to get out, because we're going to be separated. And then they were afraid because we asked once if somebody is really sick or whatever, what do they do with the people. And they said, they're getting back to Auschwitz in the-- in the right place. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so I went out of there. But in between, in Plaszow, oh, there was other things going on. They were going on shooting thing too. And there was a ditch not far away, at that hill, where things-- I was working there, and by the lumber too. It was not always the same thing. And once we were kind of carrying big, big, this-- this thick of lumber, and we were five girls in between, because they were kind of really heavy. This is where we were five, and we picked up that piece of lumber. But in the meantime, there was a guy with a dog, and sometimes he had a bicycle. And he was chasing up the hill. It was so muddy that the shoes, sometimes the mud poured down. You know, not mine, because mine was really good. I really thank for my father. But they had the problem. And by the time he got to that lumber place, ready to stack the lumber, there were just two of us, or sometimes in the middle, one, because if it was really, really a long one. And it came up the hill. He decided that fast we should run with it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one day, we-- I remember, when something, they were shooting there. They said they catch the partisans, or was it [INAUDIBLE]. For us, somebody said that it's American civilians. They found out they couldn't go back to the United States or whatever. I don't know if they were Jewish or what, but we saw them.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were in black dressed. I don't-- they-- I-- they had a name, but I don't remember what they called. And they had those little tiny things, kind of, it was just this big, the gun on the shoulder. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then they told us we should get the pieces of leftover wood or whatever, branches, and there was a little ditch. I have it on that piece of paper there. And we should leave that there. And-- and then when it came that they were going to bring them there, I suppose, and-- and it was a fire. But when they did that, they told us nobody could stay. Suddenly, everybody had to be-- whoever was in the barracks, in the barracks, and whoever worked outside, we hid behind the lumber. They couldn't-- nobody dared to stand up and to look. I mean, it was not allowed. They gave us an order. And the smell was horrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it? What was it that--

speaker: interviewee
text: A bone smell. It was bone smell. It was-- we lived on [? McArthur ?] Boulevard, and in that time, there were those incinerators. And right there, kind of like in the middle of the block. How many times there the incinerator was going, I thought, I'm going to die. That smell. I had to close the windows. It's not that the smoke came in, it was kind of that-- that feeling, that bone burning. It was terrible, terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any of the inmates that you were with die during the period that you were there in Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. No, we--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were any-- were they-- were anyone beaten?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beaten. Well, I was beaten myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: In that camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: In that camp. Yeah, because-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] twice I was beaten. The first time I was beaten, there was a men's barrack, and-- and it had, kind of, like a fence. An iron fence and a gate. I don't think it was electricity, and it, it's not that kind of a fence. Some-- I really don't remember. But it was no electricity. And there was always a guy staying by the gate when they were home. I don't know where they were working. Maybe they were for Schindler, working. We don't know or what, because we never heard of that name there. Or maybe if we heard, we didn't pay attention. And they had water. They worked kind of like-- they had, I think, a better kitchen, or better food or whatever, because they were-- really looked kind of like well-fed people. And they were in civilian dress, like-- like boots, with kind of like almost like the Germans-- surrounded all those pants, like horseback riding. You know, that stuff. And they had water there. And then we found out that when they're not there, and nobody stays by the gate, and the gate is open, and we could go in to that place where they have water, that's kind of like by the men's room. I don't remember if it was by a men's room. But there was a lot of them there, kind of like sinks.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we came home earlier once from-- my niece-- I took in the morning from my nieces' undershirts to wash them and mine and daughter had-- she just had her own one. But by the time I finished three of them and came out, and that gate was closed. And the man was then already there. He was Jewish, the man, I think. And I saw already that I am in trouble, but I thought, I'm going to explain him what it is, thing. So he said, what are you doing here? I said, we don't have any water, and I-- they told me I could come-- I just-- well, I had been told that I could come here and wash. He starts hitting me over the face. On one side and the other side. I was kind of like got dizzy a few times, and then he let me go. This the end of tape number 4.
